caveats: a: The staffing mix is a secondary presentation attributed to BLS and covers all Office Supplies and Stationery Retailers, not only qualifying recurring B2B operators.
a: Observed general-purpose AI task coverage is not the same as technically feasible substitution, and neither directly measures this industry.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input is measured at ancestor 44-45 with a 2024-wage/2022-receipts vintage mismatch and LOW quality, so it may not represent the narrowed service-heavy lens.
rho: The range is judgment rather than a measured adoption cohort.
rho: Large-chain investments and systems are not representative of independent LMM operators.
rho: This primitive applies only to the exposed opportunity in a and excludes demand, pricing, and valuation effects.
e: No source reports the share of LMM firms meeting the recurring outsourced-service lens.
e: The public-company example may overstate contract-channel prevalence among independents.
e: The frozen n input is a declared dataset gap and will be MISSING, so no firm-count claim is made here.
s5: BizBuySell transactions are self-reported and mostly below the specified EBITDA band.
s5: ODP acquisition disclosures show strategy and deal size, not a population transfer rate.
s5: Age-tab availability in the Annual Business Survey does not establish an industry-specific transfer probability.
q: A single large public operator is not representative of independent LMM contract structures.
q: Segment operating margin includes many costs and is not a direct measure of gross-benefit retention.
q: The estimate excludes volume loss and implementation difficulty, which are captured elsewhere.
d5: Reported sales mix nominal price, store closures, share shifts, and quantity; only Circana states a unit-demand change.
d5: The 2022-to-2027 horizon implicit in some public forecasts does not match the requested current-to-year-five window.
d5: Adjacent janitorial, breakroom, furniture, and hospitality products may not all be classified consistently within NAICS 459410.
o: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the narrowed lens.

o: This estimate excludes demand shrinkage already represented in d5.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, public-sector, and education customers buy physical supplies and adjacent products through repeat orders or contracts, with revenue primarily recognized per product delivery; qualifying firms add recurring account management, replenishment, print, fulfillment, or related services. Product margin, delivery economics, and negotiated account pricing dominate rather than a pure recurring software fee.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to recurring B2B managed-supplies, print, replenishment, and fulfillment relationships because NAICS 459410 principally describes merchandise retail, while storefront and one-off e-commerce sales are not an outsourced service. This is a coherence restriction, not an attractiveness selection.
lens.excluded_activities: Storefront-only or e-commerce-only one-off merchandise sales, hobby or consumer stationery boutiques without recurring service, pure product resellers without an accountable managed-service relationship, captive procurement units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms whose office-products operations include recurring external-customer service: contracted B2B workplace-supplies procurement and replenishment, managed print, recurring copy/print fulfillment, and associated accountable customer service and delivery coordination.

a.bridge: The industry staffing proxy reports sales-related occupations at 55% of employment, including retail salespersons at 37% and cashiers at 9%, plus office and administrative support at 11% and transportation/material moving at 9%. O*NET lists retail-sales tasks spanning customer discovery, product recommendation, payments, stock requisition, records, special orders, and contracts. I assigned high exposure to records, routine inquiry, quoting, catalog search, and order administration; medium exposure to assisted selling and inventory planning; and low exposure to physical stocking, delivery, security, and relationship work, then applied a wage-weighted judgment. The proxy covers the whole retail industry rather than the narrowed B2B service lens and likely understates its administrative exposure while overstating automatable cashier work.
caveats: The staffing mix is a secondary presentation attributed to BLS and covers all Office Supplies and Stationery Retailers, not only qualifying recurring B2B operators.
Observed general-purpose AI task coverage is not the same as technically feasible substitution, and neither directly measures this industry.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input is measured at ancestor 44-45 with a 2024-wage/2022-receipts vintage mismatch and LOW quality, so it may not represent the narrowed service-heavy lens.
a.change_evidence: A firm-level payroll extract by occupation and wage, paired with time-and-motion samples for account service, inside sales, purchasing, order entry, print estimating, warehouse, and delivery roles, would materially tighten the range; task-level Claude or Microsoft Copilot telemetry for these exact roles would move the base.
a.rationale: AI can substitute portions of product search, routine account inquiries, quote and proposal drafting, order entry, record maintenance, replenishment recommendations, and demand forecasting. Most physical fulfillment, store work, complex account negotiation, exception handling, and accountable customer relationships remain human, so exposed current work is meaningful but not dominant.

d5.bridge: Circana reports that US office-supplies revenue across physical and digital retail fell 5% in 2024 while unit demand fell 2%; ODP reports B2B sales down 8% in 2024 and 7% in the first half of 2025, with lower business-customer spending. I use these recent quantity and sales trends, moderated because the five-year lens includes replenishment, print, fulfillment, and adjacent workplace categories, to estimate the 2031-to-2026 constant-price, constant-quality quantity ratio. The branded retail data include consumers and companies outside the narrowed lens, while ODP's assortment and scale differ from independents; recent declines may overstate a five-year structural decline if demand stabilizes.
caveats: Reported sales mix nominal price, store closures, share shifts, and quantity; only Circana states a unit-demand change.
The 2022-to-2027 horizon implicit in some public forecasts does not match the requested current-to-year-five window.
Adjacent janitorial, breakroom, furniture, and hospitality products may not all be classified consistently within NAICS 459410.
d5.change_evidence: Circana unit volumes by B2B channel and product category through 2026-2028, Census AIES constant-dollar revenue for NAICS 459410, renewal volumes from independent dealer cohorts, and customer-level office-occupancy and print-meter data would materially move the range.
d5.rationale: Digitization, fewer printed documents, direct e-commerce, and cautious business spending pressure traditional supplies and transactional print. Recurring consumables, education, furniture, cleaning and breakroom adjacency, managed print, fulfillment, and some office attendance preserve demand. The base case is a moderate five-year quantity decline rather than disappearance.

caveats: No source reports the share of LMM firms meeting the recurring outsourced-service lens.
The public-company example may overstate contract-channel prevalence among independents.
The frozen n input is a declared dataset gap and will be MISSING, so no firm-count claim is made here.
e.change_evidence: A business-universe file for NAICS 459410 linked to websites, customer mix, contract/repeat-order share, service revenue, EBITDA, and independence status would identify the eligible numerator; dealer-group member rosters and 20 broker interviews could provide a practical validation sample.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is fundamentally merchandise retail, so many firms are storefront or transactional sellers outside the outsourced-service lens. A minority plausibly serves repeat business accounts with managed replenishment, print, fulfillment, or contracted account service, as demonstrated by the leading public operator and its acquired regional dealers. Eligibility is therefore estimated below one-half.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 44-45 ($651,293,900,629) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 44-45 ($6,850,896,411,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 44-45. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1011 (margins.json[45]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (General) EBITDA/Sales 10.11%); revenue/employee $430,267 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 44-45); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the narrowed lens.
Operator need varies sharply between physical fulfillment, same-day print, furniture installation, and commoditized supplies.
This estimate excludes demand shrinkage already represented in d5.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement surveys measuring direct-manufacturer, marketplace, self-service, and managed-dealer shares; loss-reason data from dealer CRMs; and contract-renewal studies separating operator displacement from channel migration would materially change o.
o.rationale: Physical sourcing, inventory, delivery, print production, installation, customer-specific pricing, service recovery, and accountable contract performance still require an operator. However, routine ordering and support can become self-service, large marketplaces and manufacturers can supply customers directly, and software can automate replenishment and transactional print workflows. The qualifying operator retains a majority, but not all, of year-five quantity.

q.bridge: ODP's 2024 filing describes point-in-time product revenue, routine competitive-price analysis, competitive pricing, and only a 3% operating margin in its B2B division; it does not measure retention of AI benefit. I translate these contract and competition characteristics into a discounted five-year retention range: near-term labor savings can be kept, but transparent product prices, bids, account renewals, and competition pass part of the benefit to customers. A large national operator may face more formal bidding than regional LMM dealers, biasing inferred pass-through upward, while its purchasing scale may bias margin resilience upward.
caveats: A single large public operator is not representative of independent LMM contract structures.
Segment operating margin includes many costs and is not a direct measure of gross-benefit retention.
The estimate excludes volume loss and implementation difficulty, which are captured elsewhere.
q.change_evidence: Contract samples showing price-adjustment and open-book clauses, bid and renewal cadence, gross-margin waterfalls by customer cohort, and realized price/mix after documented automation projects would materially move q; customer interviews should test whether service-level gains are paid for or expected free.
q.rationale: Most revenue is tied to delivered products rather than explicit labor billing, allowing initial labor savings to remain inside gross profit. Yet standardized catalogs, comparable online prices, negotiated B2B contracts, low operating margins, and renewal competition should transmit a substantial share through lower prices or better service over five years.

caveats: The range is judgment rather than a measured adoption cohort.
Large-chain investments and systems are not representative of independent LMM operators.
This primitive applies only to the exposed opportunity in a and excludes demand, pricing, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Dated implementation case studies from independent office-products dealers showing pre/post labor hours, error rates, service levels, and adoption across CRM, ERP, e-commerce, print estimating, and customer support would materially move this estimate; interviews with 15-20 qualifying operators and their software vendors would test implementation capacity.
rho.rationale: Within five years, routine inquiry, content, quote, order, and planning workflows should be implementable through existing commerce, CRM, and inventory systems. Realization is constrained by fragmented small-firm data, catalog and pricing complexity, customer-specific contracts, integration cost, hallucination and service-quality risk, employee adoption, and the need for human exception ownership.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell reports active, completed transactions for broad independent retail and the overall US small-business-for-sale market, while ODP reports repeated acquisitions of regional office-supply distribution businesses, generally at $5 million to $15 million purchase prices. I use those observations only to establish that a transfer market exists, then bound a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability below one-third. Marketplace data skew to Main Street businesses below the target EBITDA band and ODP's proprietary program may overstate transfer incidence for attractive regional dealers, making the direction of combined bias uncertain.
caveats: BizBuySell transactions are self-reported and mostly below the specified EBITDA band.
ODP acquisition disclosures show strategy and deal size, not a population transfer rate.
Age-tab availability in the Annual Business Survey does not establish an industry-specific transfer probability.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and broker transaction records tagged to NAICS 459410, matched to a denominator of eligible LMM firms and coded for control transfer versus closure or internal succession, would directly estimate s5; owner-age and succession-plan fields from the Census Annual Business Survey would refine the hazard.
s5.rationale: Independent retailers do transact, and a strategic buyer has assembled more than 20 regional office-supply distributors, but ordinary listings are much smaller than the target EBITDA band and declining demand can leave owner-dependent firms unsalable. A one-in-five five-year control-transfer probability is a cautious central case rather than a measured cohort rate.

principal_driver: Automatable account-service, sales-support, ordering, and planning work inside recurring B2B customer relationships.
principal_weakness: A product-retail NAICS with shrinking traditional demand, a minority qualifying service model, and strong price transparency.
